title: Uint8ClampedArray.from(finalPixels) copies full master pixel buffer on every profileConvergenceCurve call
description: At line 291, `refImg` is built via `Uint8ClampedArray.from(finalPixels)` which copies the entire final-frame RGBA pixel buffer (up to ~100+ MB for a 12MP image). This copy is done once per level call, which is acceptable, but `finalPixels` at line 285 is itself already a copy made inside `decodeFinal` at line 249 (`new Uint8Array(raw)`). So the reference buffer is copied twice: once in decodeFinal and once for the ssim refImg. The second copy could be avoided by constructing the refImg's data directly from the decoded ArrayBuffer without the intermediate `new Uint8Array` allocation inside decodeFinal.

title: Uint8ClampedArray.from(px) allocates a full-frame copy on every progressive pass for SSIM
description: Inside the progressive decode event loop (called once per JXL pass, potentially 10-20 times per level), `Uint8ClampedArray.from(px)` on line 327 copies the entire pass pixel buffer to build the ssim input image. For a 12MP image this is ~50 MB per pass, so 10 passes = ~500 MB of transient allocations that must be GC'd. Since ssim.js accepts ImageData-like objects, and the data only needs to be readable during the synchronous ssim call, a reusable scratch buffer (allocated once to max expected size and filled with `set()`) would eliminate these allocations entirely.

title: probeOrientation called twice for JPG path: once in computeIngestPlan and once in buildFallbackPlan
description: In `computeIngestPlan` at lines 343-345, `buildJpgLadder` is called and then `probeOrientation(bytes)` is called again to override the orientation. For the Tier-3 fallback path in `buildFallbackPlan` at line 554, `probeOrientation(jpeg)` is called again on the embedded JPEG. Each `probeOrientation` call does a full dynamic import of `exifr` and an EXIF parse of the image bytes. The result is not cached between these call sites. For a large JPEG, this EXIF parse is cheap relative to encode, but it is redundant work that could be eliminated by returning orientation from the first call site.

title: removeOrphans stat()s each orphan candidate serially inside a sequential for-loop
description: In `removeOrphans`, the loop at lines 1015-1029 iterates over all level files sequentially. For each orphan candidate it calls `await stat(full)` to check the grace window. With many orphan levels (e.g. after a batch failure on a large corpus), this serial stat pattern can be slow. The loop also calls `await unlink(full)` serially. Wrapping these in `pMapLimit` with a bounded concurrency (e.g. 16) would yield meaningful wall-time improvements on large collections.

title: removeOrphans step 3 (empty image dirs) calls fileExists() serially for each image id
description: The third loop in `removeOrphans` (lines 1032-1045) iterates over all image IDs sequentially and calls `await fileExists(mp)` then `await rm()` serially. In a gallery with thousands of images this is a significant wall-time bottleneck because each iteration is one round trip to the filesystem. This loop could be parallelised via `pMapLimit` in the same way that the manifest-scanning loop (step 1) is already bounded.

title: rebuildIndex calls fileExists() then readFile() per image — two stat/open syscalls instead of one
description: In `rebuildIndex`, the parallel map at line 941 first calls `await fileExists(manifestPath)` (which internally calls `access()`), and then if that succeeds calls `await readFile(manifestPath, 'utf8')`. This is a double syscall (access + open/read) that can be collapsed into a single `readFileOrNull` call, which is already implemented and used elsewhere in the module (e.g. validate.ts:55, ingest.ts:449). The `fileExists` guard here adds a TOCTOU window and an extra syscall for every image in the gallery.

title: f.replace(/\.jxl$/, "") uses a regex inside the orphan-scan loop
description: In `removeOrphans`, the orphan scan loop uses `f.replace(/\.jxl$/, "")` to strip the extension for each level file in the directory. For a large gallery with many level files this constructs a regex on every iteration (in V8 the literal regex is cached, but the replace call still has overhead). A simple `f.slice(0, -4)` is already used in validate.ts line 105 for the identical pattern and is marginally faster. This is low severity but is an inconsistency that can trigger slightly higher allocation in tight loops.

title: index.json written with JSON.stringify(index, null, 2) — pretty-printed for a machine-read file
description: In `rebuildIndex`, the gallery index is serialized with two-space indentation (`JSON.stringify(index, null, 2)`). For a gallery with 10 000 images each index entry is roughly 100-150 bytes of JSON, so the index could be 1-1.5 MB. Pretty-printing adds ~20-30% overhead in file size and serialization time. The manifest files are already written compact (`JSON.stringify(state)` in checkpoint.ts:76). The index is a machine-read file; compact serialization would reduce I/O on both write and subsequent reads (validate, client fetch). The runlog at cli.ts:472 also uses pretty-print (`JSON.stringify(arr, null, 2)`).

title: cpState.completed.reduce() on the entire completed array at batch end — O(N) over all prior-run entries
description: At lines 756-758 in the in-process path (and similarly at lines 926-928 in the worker path), `cpState.completed.reduce()` iterates over the entire `cpState.completed` array to sum `stagedBytes`. When `--resume` is used, `cpState.completed` includes entries from prior runs loaded from the checkpoint file. The reduce therefore walks entries from previous runs that are not part of this run's activity, inflating the total unnecessarily. A running sum updated in the per-image success handler would be O(1) total and would naturally exclude prior-run entries.
code_excerpt:
const s = cpState.completed.reduce((sum: number, c: any) => sum + (c.stagedBytes || 0), 0);
if (s > 0) result.totalStagedBytes = s;

title: Object spread on opts for every dispatched worker job copies the entire IngestOptions record
description: In the worker-pool dispatcher at line 881, `const jobOpts: any = { ...opts, statMap: undefined, statEntry: ..., idMap: undefined, imageId: preId }` creates a fresh object with all of IngestOptions' fields for every dispatched job. The opts object can carry fields like statMap (stripped here, but the spread still copies all other keys). With high concurrency and many images this creates many short-lived objects. A purpose-built minimal job options type that only carries the fields workers need would reduce allocator pressure and postMessage serialization overhead.
code_excerpt:
const preId = (opts as any).imageId || ((opts as any).idMap && (opts as any).idMap[path]) || imageId;
const jobOpts: any = { ...opts, statMap: undefined, statEntry: (opts as any).statMap?.[path], idMap: undefined, imageId: preId };

title: collectInputs performs a second parallel stat() on every candidate after already obtaining isDirectory() via withFileTypes
description: `collectInputs` uses `readdir(p, { withFileTypes: true })` (line 31) to avoid a stat just to test isDirectory, which is correct. However it then runs a bounded parallel `stat()` on every file candidate (line 52-54) to capture size and mtimeMs. The `Dirent` object from `withFileTypes` does not expose mtime, so a stat is genuinely needed. However, the `stat` is issued on all candidates in a single pMapLimit batch regardless of whether the downstream skip-check (manifest mtime) would eliminate most of them. For large galleries this stat burst can be reduced by deferring per-file stat to the point of need inside ingestImage, at the cost of losing the pre-collected statMap optimisation.

title: No per-level profiling breakdowns for the two-decode cost of profileConvergenceCurve
description: The `profileConvergence` and `profileConvergenceCurve` backends emit a single stage event covering the full measurement duration. There is no breakdown of: (1) decodeFinal duration, (2) progressive decode duration, (3) per-pass metric computation time. This makes it impossible to distinguish whether the cost is dominated by the double-decode or by the per-pass SSIM/Butteraugli computation, which is the information needed to decide whether caching the final-ref decode across levels would actually help. Adding per-phase timing to the existing `tel?.stage` calls would be a zero-cost improvement to observability.

title: No ingest throughput benchmark; performance regressions in encode/decode paths are invisible until production
description: The test suite (ingest.test.ts, tiling-ingest.test.ts) exercises correctness but does not measure wall-time or throughput. The codebase has a benchmark infrastructure in `benchmark/` and `run-10-benchmarks.mjs` for the WASM encode path, but no benchmark covers the pyramid-ingest Node.js pipeline (collect → stat → decode → ladder → write). A single synthetic benchmark (e.g. 10 fake 12MP images, measure total ingest time + per-image ms) would catch regressions in the hot path (readdir, pMapLimit tuning, existingLevels Set construction, etc.) before they reach production.
code_excerpt: // No benchmark file exists under packages/pyramid-ingest/

title: --explain with imageId stat()s each level file serially to count on-disk presence
description: In the `--explain` path for a hex imageId, lines 173-179 loop over every level in the manifest and call `await stat(join(levelsDir, hash + '.jxl'))` serially to count how many are present on disk. For a manifest with 5 levels this is 5 serial round-trips. Using `Promise.all` or pre-fetching a directory listing would reduce it to a single `readdir` + Set lookup, consistent with the pattern in `writeLevelFiles` (existingLevels Set) and `validate`.
